summary:
Abu Hureyra Phase 2 refers to the later Neolithic occupation of the site of Abu Hureyra on the Euphrates River in northern Syria, dating from approximately 7500–6000 BCE. In this phase the settlement grew substantially, eventually covering around 11 hectares and housing an estimated population of several thousand — making it one of the largest early Neolithic settlements known. Rectangular mudbrick houses replaced the earlier pit-dwelling architecture, and the community practiced a fully agricultural economy based on domesticated cereals, legumes, sheep, and goats.

The skeletal remains from Abu Hureyra Phase 2 have been the subject of detailed biomechanical analysis that has documented the physical stresses of early agricultural life. The spinal and joint deformities visible in many individuals are consistent with the repetitive labor demands of grinding grain, making Abu Hureyra one of the most informative sites for understanding the human costs of the transition to farming. The site was submerged by the Tabqa Dam reservoir in the 1970s after extensive rescue excavations.
